title: Mount Elastic File System (EFS) on EC2

tasks.title: Task 1: Sign in to AWS Management Console

steps.text: Click on the Open Console button, and you will get redirected to AWS Console in a new browser tab.

On the AWS sign-in page, Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12 digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab. Now copy your User Name and Password in the Lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
On the AWS sign-in page,
Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12 digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab.
Now copy your User Name and Password in the Lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Once Signed In to the AWS Management Console, Make the default AWS Region as US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1.
Note : If you face any issues, please go through FAQs and Troubleshooting for Labs.
Make sure you are in the N.Virginia Region.
Navigate to the Services menu in the top, then click on EC2 in the Compute section.
Click on Instances from the left side bar and then click on Launch instances.
Number of Instances : Enter 2 on the right side under summary
Name : Enter MyEC2
For Amazon Machine Image (AMI): Search for Amazon Linux 2023 kernel-6.1 AMI in the search box and click on the Select button.
For Instance Type: select t2.micro
Task 2: Launching two EC2 Instances
For Key pair: Select Create a new key pair Button Key pair name: MyEC2Key Key pair type: RSA Private key file format: .pem
For Key pair: Select Create a new key pair Button
Key pair name: MyEC2Key
Key pair type: RSA
Private key file format: .pem
Select Create key pair Button.
In Network Settings Click on Edit:
Auto-assign public IP: Enable
Select Create new Security group
Security Group Name: Enter EFS Security Group To add SSH: Choose Type: SSH Source: Anywhere For NFS: Click on Add security group rule Choose Type: NFS Source: Anywhere
Security Group Name: Enter EFS Security Group
To add SSH: Choose Type: SSH Source: Anywhere
To add SSH:
Choose Type: SSH
Source: Anywhere
For NFS: Click on Add security group rule Choose Type: NFS Source: Anywhere
For NFS:
Click on Add security group rule
Choose Type: NFS
Source: Anywhere
Keep Rest thing Default and Click on Launch Instance Button.
Select View all Instances to View Instance you Created
Launch Status: Your instance is now launching. Click on the instance ID and wait until the initialization status changes to Running.
Click on each instance and enter a names as MyEC2-1 and MyEC2-2.
Task 2: Launching two EC2 Instances
Take note of the IPv4 Public IP Addresses of the EC2 instances and save them for later.

tasks.title: Task 3: Creating an Elastic File System

steps.text: Navigate to EFS by clicking on the Services menu at the top. Click on EFS in the Storage section.
Click on Create file system
Click on Customize button.
Enter the details below, Type the Name as EFS-Demo and make sure default VPC and default Regional options are selected.
Task 3: Creating an Elastic File System
Uncheck the option of Enable automated backups
Leave everything by default and click on the Next button present below.
Network Access:
VPC An Amazon EFS file system is accessed by EC2 instances running inside one of your VPCs. Choose the same VPC you selected while launching the EC2 instance (leave as default).
VPC
An Amazon EFS file system is accessed by EC2 instances running inside one of your VPCs.
Choose the same VPC you selected while launching the EC2 instance (leave as default).

Mount Targets Instances connect to a file system by using a network interface called a mount target. Each mount target has an IP address, which we assign automatically or you can specify. We will select all the Availability Zones (AZ’s) so that the EC2 instances across your VPC can access the file system. Select all the Availability Zones, and in the Security Groups, select EFS Security Group instead of the default value.
Scroll down to 'Mount Targets', then click on 'Add Mount Target'. Select the availability zone and change the security group to EFS Security Group. Make sure you remove the default security group and select the EFS Security Group, otherwise you will get an error in further steps. Click on Next button
Mount Targets
Instances connect to a file system by using a network interface called a mount target. Each mount target has an IP address, which we assign automatically or you can specify.
We will select all the Availability Zones (AZ’s) so that the EC2 instances across your VPC can access the file system.
Select all the Availability Zones, and in the Security Groups, select EFS Security Group instead of the default value.
Scroll down to 'Mount Targets', then click on 'Add Mount Target'. Select the availability zone and change the security group to EFS Security Group.
Make sure you remove the default security group and select the EFS Security Group, otherwise you will get an error in further steps.
Click on Next button
Task 3: Creating an Elastic File System
File system policy - optional let it be optional only. Click on Next button
Review and Create: Review the configuration below before proceeding to create your file system. Click on Create button.
Congratulations on creating the EFS File system, It's time to mount your EC2 Instance with the EFS File system.
Task 3: Creating an Elastic File System

tasks.title: Task 4: Mount the File System to MyEC2-1 Instance

steps.text: Select the MyEC2-1 Instance and copy the IPv4 Public IP.
SSH into the EC2 Instance
Please follow the steps in SSH into EC2 Instance.
Switch to root usersudo -s
Switch to root user
Run the updates using the following command: yum -y update
Run the updates using the following command:
Install the NFS client as amazon-efs-utils.yum install -y amazon-efs-utils
Install the NFS client as amazon-efs-utils.
Create a directory by the name efsmkdir efs
Create a directory by the name efs
We have to mount the file system in this directory.
To do so, navigate to the AWS console and click on the created file system. On the top-right corner, click on Attach
Copy the command of Using the EFS mount helper.sudo mount -t efs -o tls <DNS Name>:/ efs
Copy the command of Using the EFS mount helper.
Note: Replace <dns name of EFS> with the DNS of the Elastic File System you've created.
To display information for all currently-mounted file systems, we'll use the command bellow:df -h
To display information for all currently-mounted file systems, we'll use the command bellow:

Create a directory in our current location:mkdir aws
Create a directory in our current location:

tasks.title: Task 5: Mount the File System to MyEC2-2 Instance

steps.text: Select the MyEC2-2 Instance and copy the IPv4 Public IP.
SSH into the EC2 Instance
Please follow the steps in SSH into EC2 Instance.
Switch to root usersudo -s
Switch to root user
Run the updates using the following command: yum -y update
Run the updates using the following command:
Install the NFS client as amazon-efs-utils.yum -y install amazon-efs-utils
Install the NFS client as amazon-efs-utils.
Create a directory with the name efsmkdir efs
Create a directory with the name efs
We have to mount the file system in this directory.
To do so, navigate to the AWS console and click on the created file system. On the top-right corner, click on Attach
Copy the command of Using the EFS mount helper.sudo mount -t efs -o tls <DNS Name>:/ efsNote: Replace <dns name of EFS> with the DNS of the Elastic File System you've created.
Copy the command of Using the EFS mount helper.
Note: Replace <dns name of EFS> with the DNS of the Elastic File System you've created.
To display information for all currently mounted file systems, we'll use the command
Task 5: Mount the File System to MyEC2-2 Instance
SSH into both instances in a side-by-side view on your machine, if possible.
Switch to root usersudo -s
Switch to root user
Navigate to the efs directory in both the servers using the commandcd efs
Navigate to the efs directory in both the servers using the command
Create a file in any one server.touch hello.txt
Create a file in any one server.
Check the file using the commandls -ltr
Check the file using the command
Now go to the other server and give the commandls -ltr
Now go to the other server and give the command
You can see the file created on this server as well. This proves that our EFS is working.
You can try creating files (touch command) or directories (mkdir command) on other servers to continue to grow the EFS implementation.
Task 6: Testing the File System
Once the lab steps are completed, please click on the Validation button on the left side panel.
This will validate the resources in the AWS account and displays whether you have completed this lab successfully or not.
Sample output :
Task 7: Validation Test
You have successfully created an Elastic File System.
You have successfully created 2 Amazon Linux instances.
You have successfully installed the NFS Client and mounted EFS to the instances.
You have successfully tested the file-share between both Instances.
Sign out of AWS Account.
You have successfully completed the lab.
Once you have completed the steps, click on End Lab from the lab console.
